draft.introduction: Ramon Llull’s Ars Magna (c. 1305) is a combinatorial method for discovering truth through the systematic combination of fundamental concepts. Llull believed this “Art” could demonstrate the truth of Christianity to Muslims and Jews through reason alone.

translation.text: God possesses in himself all perfections: goodness, greatness, eternity, power, wisdom, will, virtue, truth, and glory. These dignities are equal to one another in God, for in God goodness is as great as greatness, and greatness is as eternal as eternity, and so with all the rest. By combining these dignities systematically, we can arrive at necessary truths about God and all of creation.
translation.original_text: [Latin: Deus in se ipso habet omnes perfectiones]

translator_notes.note: Llull’s combinatorial method is often considered a precursor to modern computing and formal logic. Leibniz explicitly acknowledged his debt to Llull. The “Art” was designed for interfaith dialogue: Llull believed that Muslims, Christians, and Jews could agree on God’s attributes (the “dignities”) and then reason together from shared premises.
